cannot be captured by the existing symmetry indicators in the literature.
Nevertheless, our Majorana counting rule does enable a correct diagnosis for
these "beyond-indicator" models. We conjecture that all Wannierizable
superconductors should yield a decomposition in terms of our Kitaev building
blocks, up to adiabatic deformations. Based on this conjecture, we propose a
universal diagnosis of higher-order topology that possibly works for all
Wannierizable superconductors. We also present a realistic example of
higher-order topological superconductors with fragile Wannier obstruction to
verify our conjectured universal diagnosis. Our work paves the way for a
complete topological theory for superconductors.

In this paper, we study the effect of population imbalance and its interplay
with pairing strength and lattice effect in atomic Fermi gases in a
one-dimensional optical lattice. We compute various phase diagrams as the
system undergoes BCS-BEC crossover, using the same pairing fluctuation theory
as in Part I. We find widespread pseudogap phenomena beyond the BCS regime and
intermediate temperature superfluid states for relatively low population
imbalances. The Fermi surface topology plays an important role in the behavior
of $T_\text{c}$. For large $d$ and/or small $t$, which yield an open Fermi
surface, superfluidity can be readily destroyed by a small amount of population
imbalance $p$. The superfluid phase, especially in the BEC regime, can exist
only for a highly restricted volume of the parameter space. Due to the
continuum-lattice mixing, population imbalance gives rise to a new mechanism
for pair hopping, as assisted by excessive majority fermions, which may lead to
significant enhancement of $T_\text{c}$ on the BEC side of the Feshbach
resonance, and also render $T_\text{c}$ approaching a constant asymptote in the
BEC limit, when it exists. Furthermore, we find that not all minority fermions
will be paired up in BEC limit, unlike the 3D continuum case. These predictions
can be tested in future experiments.

We consider the convexity properties of distortion functionals, particularly
the linear distortion, defined for homeomorphisms of domains in Euclidean
$n$-spaces, $n\geq 3$. The inner and outer distortion functionals are lower
semi-continuous in all dimensions and so for the curve modulus or analytic
definitions of quasiconformality it ifollows that if $ \{ f_{n}
\}_{n=1}^{\infty} $ is a sequence of $K$-quasiconformal mappings (here $K$
depends on the particular distortion functional but is the same for every
element of the sequence) which converges locally uniformly to a mapping $f$,
then this limit function is also $K$-quasiconformal.Despite a widespread belief
that this was also true for the geometric definition of quasiconformality
(defined through the linear distortion $H({f_{n}})$), T. Iwaniec gave a
specific and surprising example to show that the linear distortion functional
is not always lower semicontinuous on uniformly converging sequences of
quasiconformal mappings. Here we show that this failure of lower semicontinuity
is common, perhaps generic in the sense that under mild restrictions on a
quasiconformal $f$, there is a sequence $ \{f_{n} \}_{n=1}^{\infty} $ with $
{f_{n}}\to {f}$ locally uniformly and with $\limsup_{n\to\infty} H( {f_{n}})<H(
{f})$. Our main result shows this is true for affine mappings. Addressing
conjectures of F.W. Gehring and Iwaniec we show the jump up in the limit can be
arbitrarily large and give conjecturally sharp bounds : for each
$\alpha<\sqrt{2}$ there is ${f_{n}}\to {f}$ locally uniformly with $f$ affine
and \[ \alpha \; \limsup_{n\to\infty} H( {f_{n}}) < H( {f}) \] We conjecture
$\sqrt{2}$ to be best possible.

The mixed spin-(1/2, S_B, S_C) Ising model on a decorated square lattice with
two different kinds of decorating spins S_B and S_C placed on its horizontal
and vertical bonds, respectively, is exactly solved by establishing a precise
mapping relationship with the corresponding spin-1/2 Ising model on an
anisotropic square (rectangular) lattice. The effect of uniaxial single-ion
anisotropy acting on both types of decorating spins S_B and S_C is examined in
particular. If decorating spins S_B and S_C are integer and half-odd-integer,
respectively, or if the reverse is the case, the model under investigation
displays a very peculiar critical behavior beared on the spontaneously ordered
'quasi-1D' spin system, which appears as a result of the single-ion anisotropy
strengthening. We have found convincing evidence that this remarkable
spontaneous ordering virtually arises even though all integer-valued decorating
spins tend towards their 'non-magnetic' spin state S=0 and the system becomes
disordered only upon further increase of the single-ion anisotropy. The
single-ion anisotropy parameter is also at an origin of various temperature
dependences of the total magnetization when imposing the pure ferrimagnetic or
the mixed ferro-ferrimagnetic character of the spin arrangement.

We investigate Cu-doped Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ with transport,
magnetic susceptibility, and elastic neutron scattering measurements. In the
heavily Cu-doped regime where long-range stripe-type antiferromagnetic order in
BaFe$_2$As$_2$ is suppressed, Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ (0.145 $\leq x
\leq$ 0.553) samples exhibit spin-glass-like behavior in magnetic
susceptibility and insulating-like temperature dependence in electrical
transport. Using elastic neutron scattering, we find stripe-type short-range
magnetic order in the spin-glass region identified by susceptibility
measurements. The persistence of short-range magnetic order over a large doping
range in Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ likely arises from local arrangements
of Fe and Cu that favor magnetic order, with Cu acting as vacancies relieving
magnetic frustration and degeneracy. These results indicate locally broken
four-fold rotational symmetry, suggesting that stripe-type magnetism is
ubiquitous in iron pnictides.

In the second half of the last century the "frozen star" or "collapsed star"
models of black hole formation were largely abandoned. The result is that later
models appear to either assume that black hole event horizons already exist
(such as in Hawking's 1975 paper on radiation from black holes), bypass the
relative frames altogether (such as in Mirabel's 2017 paper on the formation of
stellar black holes), or use coordinate systems that essentially ignore the
remote viewer's point of view (Kruskal 1960).
  This paper attempts to re-establish the concept of a "nascent black hole" as
the correct approach for modelling black holes from remote reference frames. It
uses, and only needs to use, Schwarzschild metrics and presents some example
scenarios to demonstrate the concepts through worked examples. Alternatives
such as Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates and Penrose's local collapsing frames
are not disputed; the Schwarzschild metrics must still be valid outside the
event horizon and this paper is largely concerned with "remote viewers" for
various meanings of "remote".
  For most astrophysical or cosmological questions the difference between
"actual" and "nascent" black holes is largely irrelevant as they behave
similarly for most practical purposes. However the difference becomes critical
for effects that require an event horizon to interact with the remote viewer,
such as Hawking-Zel'dovich radiation and the related information problems
outlined by Susskind. That is: if there are no possible observable paths from a
nascent event horizon to any remote viewer then there also cannot be any
evaporation to the remote viewer

This paper studies the SI1SI2S spreading model of two competing behaviors
over a bilayer network. We address the problem of determining resource
allocation strategies which design a spreading network so as to ensure the
extinction of a selected process. Our discussion begins by extending the
SI1SI2S model to edge-dependent infection and node-dependent recovery
parameters with generalized graph topologies, which builds upon prior work that
studies the homogeneous case. We then find conditions under which the
mean-field approximation of a chosen epidemic process stabilizes to extinction
exponentially quickly. Leveraging this result, we formulate and solve an
optimal resource allocation problem in which we minimize the expenditure
necessary to force a chosen epidemic process to become extinct as quickly as
possible. In the case that the budget is not sufficient to ensure extinction of
the desired process, we instead minimize a useful heuristic. We explore the
efficacy of our methods by comparing simulations of the stochastic process to
the mean-field model, and find that the mean-field methods developed work well
for the optimal cost networks designed, but suffer from inaccuracy in other
situations.

Classifications of symmetries and conservation laws are presented for a
variety of physically and analytically interesting wave equations with power
onlinearities in n spatial dimensions: a radial hyperbolic equation, a radial
Schrodinger equation and its derivative variant, and two proposed radial
generalizations of modified Korteweg--de Vries equations, as well as
Hamiltonian variants. The mains results classify all admitted local point
symmetries and all admitted local conserved densities depending on up to first
order spatial derivatives, including any that exist only for special powers or
dimensions. All such cases for which these wave equations admit, in particular,
dilational energies or conformal energies and inversion symmetries are
determined. In addition, potential systems arising from the classified
conservation laws are used to determine nonlocal symmetries and nonlocal
conserved quantities admitted by these equations. As illustrative applications,
a discussion is given of energy norms, conserved H^s norms, critical powers for
blow-up solutions, and one-dimensional optimal symmetry groups for invariant
solutions.

Cancers are caused by the accumulation of genetic alterations. Since this
accumulation takes time, the incidence of most cancers is thought to increase
exponentially with age. However, careful measurements of the age-specific
incidence shows that the specific incidence for many forms of cancer rises with
age to a maximum, then decreases. This decrease in the age-specific incidence
with age is an anomaly. Understanding this anomaly should lead to a better
understanding of how tumors develop and grow. Here I derive the shape of the
age-specific incidence, showing that it should follow the shape of a Weibull
distribution. Measurements indicate that the age-specific incidence for colon
cancer does indeed follow a Weibull distribution. This analysis leads to the
interpretation that for colon cancer two sub-populations exist in the general
population: a susceptible population and an immune population. Colon tumors
will only occur in the susceptible population. This analysis is consistent with
the developmental origins of disease hypothesis and generalizable to many other
common forms of cancer.

Recent photometric and polarimetric observations of type Ia supernovae (SNe
Ia) show unusually low total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_{V}<2$) and
wavelength of maximum polarization ($\lambda_{max}<0.4\mu m$) for several SNe
Ia, which indicates peculiar properties of interstellar (IS) dust in the SN
hosted galaxies and/or the presence of circumstellar (CS) dust. In this paper,
we use inversion technique to infer best-fit grain size distribution and
alignment function of interstellar grains along the lines of sight toward four
SNe Ia with anomalous extinction and polarization data (SNe 1986G, 2006X,
2008fp, and 2014J). We find that to reproduce low values of $R_{V}$, a
significant enhancement in the mass of small grains of radius $a< 0.1\mu m$ is
required. For SN 2014J, a simultaneous fit to observed extinction and
polarization data is unsuccessful if the entire data is attributed to IS dust
(model 1), but a good fit is obtained when accounting for the contribution of
CS dust (model 2). For SN 2008fp, our fitting results for model 1 show that, to
reproduce an extreme value of $\lambda_{\max}\sim 0.15\mu m$, very small
silicate grains must be aligned as efficiently as big grains. We suggest that
tiny grains in the intervening molecular cloud can be aligned efficiently by
radiative torques (RATs) from the SNe Ia. The resulting time dependence
polarization from this RAT alignment model can be tested by observing at
ultraviolet wavelengths. Our results are in favor of the existence of CS dust
in SN 2014J, but its presence in SN 2008fp remains uncertain.

Simplified assumptions about the thermodynamics of the electrons are normally
employed in general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of
accretion onto black holes. To counter this, we have developed a
self-consistent approach to study magnetised and radiatively cooled
two-temperature accretion flows around a Kerr black hole in two spatial
dimensions. The approach includes several heating processes, radiative cooling,
and a coupling between the electrons and the ions via Coulomb interaction. We
test our approach by performing axisymmetric GRMHD simulations of magnetised
tori accreting onto a Kerr black hole under various astrophysical scenarios. In
this way, we find that the inclusion of the Coulomb interaction and the
radiative cooling impacts the thermodynamical properties of both the ions and
electrons, changing significantly the temperature distribution of the latter,
and underlining the importance of a two-temperature approach when imaging these
flows. In addition, we find that the accretion rate influences the bulk
properties of the flow as well as the thermodynamics of the electrons and ions.
Interestingly, we observe qualitatively distinct temperature properties for
SANE and MAD accretion modes while maintaining the same accretion rates, which
could help distinguishing MAD and SANE accretion flows via observations.
Finally, we propose two new relations for the temperature ratios of the
electrons, ions, and of the gas in terms of the plasma-$\beta$ parameter. The
new relations represent a simple and effective approach to treat
two-temperature accretion flows on supermassive black holes such as Sgr A* and
M\,87*.

Motivated by recent experiments, we study the variability of the thermal
conductance of single dithiolated alkane molecules of varying length connecting
two gold electrodes. For this purpose, we examine (i) the influence of the
metal-molecule contact and of the electrode orientation on the thermal
conductance of straight alkane chains, (ii) the effect of molecule-internal
disorder realized through torsional gauche defects and the behavior upon
stretching, and (iii) the modifications resulting from temperature-dependent
dynamical variations of the geometry. While we analyze the former two aspects
with a combination of density functional theory (DFT) and nonequilibrium
Green's function (NEGF) methods, in the latter case we use nonequilibrium
molecular dynamics (NEMD). Our calculations show the size of the variation of
the phonon thermal conductance due to changes in contact geometry, that gauche
defects generally reduce the thermal conductance but that they vanish upon
stretching of the junction, and that the thermal conductance at elevated
temperatures results from an average over many junction configurations,
including alkanes with thermally excited gauche defects. Overall we find a good
agreement between the DFT-NEGF and NEMD approaches for our room-temperature
investigations. This confirms that anharmonic effects do not play a major role
in alkane chains containing between four to ten carbon atoms. The phonon heat
transport thus proceeds elastically and phase-coherently through these short
molecular junctions.

A scalable quantum information processing architecture based on silicon
metal-oxide-semiconductor technology is presented, combining quantum hardware
elements from planar and 3D silicon-on-insulator technologies. This
architecture is expressed in the ``unit cell'' approach, where tiling cells in
two dimensions and allowing inter-cellular nearest-neighbour interactions makes
the architecture compatible with the surface code for fault tolerant quantum
computation. The architecture utilises global control methods, substantially
reducing processor complexity with scale: Single-qubit control is achieved
using globally applied spin-resonance techniques and two-qubit interactions are
mediated by large quantum dots. Further, a solution to device variation is
proposed through integration of electronics for individual trimming of quantum
dot voltage references. Such a combined set of solutions addresses several
major barriers to scaling quantum machines within completely silicon based
architectures.

The origin of solar transition region redshifts is not completely understood.
Current research is addressing this issue by investigating three-dimensional
magneto-hydrodynamic models that extend from the photosphere to the corona. By
studying the average properties of emission line profiles synthesized from the
simulation runs and comparing them to observations with present-day
instrumentation, we investigate the origin of mass flows in the solar
transition region and corona. Doppler shifts were determined from the emission
line profiles of various extreme-ultraviolet emission lines formed in the range
of $T=10^4-10^6$ K. Plasma velocities and mass flows were investigated for
their contribution to the observed Doppler shifts in the model. In particular,
the temporal evolution of plasma flows along the magnetic field lines was
analyzed. Comparing observed vs. modeled Doppler shifts shows a good
correlation in the temperature range $\log(T$/[K])=4.5-5.7, which is the basis
of our search for the origin of the line shifts. The vertical velocity obtained
when weighting the velocity by the density squared is shown to be almost
identical to the corresponding Doppler shift. Therefore, a direct comparison
between Doppler shifts and the model parameters is allowed. A simple
interpretation of Doppler shifts in terms of mass flux leads to overestimating
the mass flux. Upflows in the model appear in the form of cool pockets of gas
that heat up slowly as they rise. Their low temperature means that these
pockets are not observed as blueshifts in the transition region and coronal
lines. For a set of magnetic field lines, two different flow phases could be
identified. The coronal part of the field line is intermittently connected to
subjacent layers of either strong or weak heating, leading either to mass flows
into the loop or to the draining of the loop.

The effect of quasi-particle (QP) 'scattering' by the vortex lattice on the
de-Haas van-Alphen oscillations in a pure type-II superconductor is
investigated within mean field,asymptotic perturbation theory. Using a 2D
electron gas model it is shown that, due to a strict phase coherence in the
many-particle correlation functions, the 'scattering' effect in the asymptotic
limit ($\sqrt{E_F/\hbar\omega_c}\gg 1$) is much weaker than what is predicted
by the random vortex lattice model proposed by Maki and Stephen, which destroys
this coherence . The coherent many particle configuration is a collinear array
of many particle coordinates, localized within a spatial region with size of
the order of the magnetic length. The amplitude of the magnetization
oscillations is sharply damped just below $% H_{c2}$ because of strong
$180^{\circ}$ out of phase magnetic oscillations in the superconducting
condensation energy ,which tend to cancel the normal electron oscillations.
Within the ideal 2D model used it is found, however, that because of the
relative smallness of the quartic and higher order terms in the expansion, the
oscillations amplitude at lower fields does not really damp to zero, but only
reverses sign and remains virtually undamped well below $H_{c2}$. This
conclusion may be changed if disorder in the vortex lattice, or vortex lines
motion will be taken into account. The reduced QP 'scattering' effect may be
responsible for the apparent crossover from a strong damping of the dHvA
oscillations just below $H_{c2}$ to a weaker damping at lower fields observed
experimentally in several 3D superconductors.

CONTEXT: Recent progress in the seismic interpretation of field beta Cep
stars has resulted in improvements of the physics in the stellar structure and
evolution models of massive stars. Further asteroseismic constraints can be
obtained from studying ensembles of stars in a young open cluster, which all
have similar age, distance and chemical composition.
  AIMS: To improve our comprehension of the beta Cep stars, we studied the
young open cluster NGC 884 to discover new B-type pulsators, besides the two
known beta Cep stars, and other variable stars.
  METHODS: An extensive multi-site campaign was set up to gather accurate CCD
photometry time series in four filters (U, B, V, I) of a field of NGC884.
Fifteen different instruments collected almost 77500 CCD images in 1286 hours.
The images were calibrated and reduced to transform the CCD frames into
interpretable differential light curves. Various variability indicators and
frequency analyses were applied to detect variable stars in the field. Absolute
photometry was taken to deduce some general cluster and stellar properties.
  RESULTS: We achieved an accuracy for the brightest stars of 5.7 mmag in V,
6.9 mmag in B, 5.0 mmag in I and 5.3 mmag in U. The noise level in the
amplitude spectra is 50 micromag in the V band. Our campaign confirms the
previously known pulsators, and we report more than one hundred new multi- and
mono-periodic B-, A- and F-type stars. Their interpretation in terms of
classical instability domains is not straightforward, pointing to imperfections
in theoretical instability computations. In addition, we have discovered six
new eclipsing binaries and four candidates as well as other irregular variable
stars in the observed field.

Recent studies indicate that predictions from machine-learning models can exacerbate biases found in the training data. When bias is amplified, the model tends to make certain predictions more frequently for specific groups than what the training data would suggest. To address this bias amplification, it is essential to have a comprehensive understanding of the underlying mechanics in contemporary machine learning that lead to such amplification. We conduct the first systematic and controlled investigation into the circumstances and mechanisms of bias amplification. For this research, we create a straightforward image-classification task that allows us to tightly control synthetic biases. Our findings reveal that the degree of bias amplification correlates with various factors, including model accuracy, capacity, overconfidence, and the volume of training data. Additionally, we observe significant fluctuations in bias amplification throughout the training process. Notably, it appears that bias amplification is influenced by the relative difficulty of the classification task compared to recognizing group membership; it tends to occur more often when identifying group membership is easier than class membership. Our findings provide guidelines for training machine-learning models, which we believe will contribute to the development of more effective bias mitigation strategies. The code for this study is available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/cv_bias_amplification.

Numerous previous studies have outlined the application of absorption spectroscopy to assess spatial temperature variations along an optical path. This is achieved by measuring the distinctive nonlinear temperature response of various molecular absorption transitions and executing an inversion. While new laser absorption spectroscopy techniques are particularly effective for this type of nonuniformity measurement, current analytical methods focus solely on isolated features rather than comprehensive broadband spectral data. In this study, we introduce a constrained spectral fitting technique named E"-binning to analyze absorption spectra from nonuniform environments. The information obtained through E"-binning is subsequently utilized in the inversion method presented in an earlier paper in this series (Malarich and Rieker, JQSRT 107455) to ascertain the temperature distribution. We illustrate this methodology using dual frequency comb laser measurements to identify convection cells within a tube furnace. The temperature distributions retrieved at various measurement heights align with an established natural convection model. Lastly, we demonstrate that for real-world measurements impacted by noise and absorption model inaccuracies, broadening the bandwidth and increasing the number of absorption transitions analyzed can enhance the precision of the temperature distribution. We have made the fitting code publicly accessible for application with any broadband absorption measurement.

The log-Gaussian Cox process represents a versatile and widely-used category of point pattern models designed to capture spatial and spatiotemporal dependencies in point patterns. Fitting these models necessitates the approximation of stochastic integrals, achieved through discretization over the relevant domain. When using fine-scale discretization, Markov chain Monte Carlo inference becomes computationally intensive due to the demands of matrix decompositions and storage, such as Cholesky decompositions, particularly with high-dimensional covariance matrices linked to latent Gaussian variables. This article tackles these computational challenges by integrating two recent advancements: (i) a data augmentation approach developed for space-time Gaussian Cox processes, which relies on precise Bayesian inference without the need for fine grid approximations for infinite-dimensional integrals, and (ii) a novel set of sparsity-inducing Gaussian processes known as nearest-neighbor Gaussian processes, which help minimize costly matrix computations. Our inference method adheres to a fully model-based Bayesian framework without compromising the complexity found in traditional log-Gaussian Cox processes. We demonstrate the application of our approach on crime event data from San Francisco, focusing on the reconstruction of the intensity surface.

In quantum mechanics, predictions are typically made by calculating the expectation values of observables represented by Hermitian operators. However, it is relatively rare to utilize non-Hermitian operators for measurement purposes. In this work, we demonstrate that the expectation values of a specific group of non-Hermitian matrices, which we refer to as column operators, can directly provide the complex coefficients of a quantum state vector. We define the state vector in terms of measurable quantities by breaking down the column operators into observables. This approach makes very-large-scale quantum states significantly easier to access in laboratory settings, as we illustrate by experimentally characterizing a 100,000-dimensional entangled state. This marks a two-order-of-magnitude enhancement over prior characterizations of discrete entangled states. Additionally, in our numerical analyses, we investigate mixed quantum states and show that for purities above 0.81, we can accurately extract the most significant eigenvector of the density matrix with a probability exceeding 99%. We believe that our method will be a valuable tool in advancing the understanding and manipulation of large-scale quantum systems.

We tackle network congestion control in datacenters through Reinforcement Learning (RL). Effective congestion control algorithms can significantly enhance latency and overall network throughput. However, to date, no learning-based algorithms have demonstrated practical applicability in this area. Currently, the most widely used solutions depend on rule-based heuristics tested against a fixed set of benchmarks, which limits their ability to adapt to new situations. In contrast, we develop an RL-based algorithm designed to generalize across various configurations of real-world datacenter networks. We address challenges including partial observability, non-stationarity, and multi-objectiveness. Additionally, we introduce a policy gradient algorithm that utilizes the analytical characteristics of the reward function to estimate its derivative, thereby enhancing stability. Our results indicate that this method surpasses other popular RL techniques and effectively adapts to scenarios not encountered during training. Experiments conducted in a realistic simulator that mimics communication networks demonstrate superior performance across multiple metrics compared to commonly used algorithms in existing datacenters. Our algorithm is currently being prepared for deployment to replace heuristics in some of the largest datacenters worldwide.

Recent studies of ridge-like structures in angular correlations of $p$-$p$ and $p$-$A$ collisions observed at RHIC and LHC have been interpreted as indications of collective motion in smaller collision systems. It has been suggested that if correlation patterns associated with flow in $A$-$A$ collisions are also present in smaller systems, then collectivity (flow) must apply to those smaller systems as well. However, this reasoning could be inverted, suggesting that such patterns observed in $A$-$A$ collisions do not necessarily indicate hydrodynamic flow. 

I present data on spectrum, correlation, and fluctuations from RHIC $p$-$p$ and Au-Au collisions, along with results from LHC $p$-$p$, $p$-Pb, and Pb-Pb collisions, which can be accurately described using a two-component (soft + dijet) model of hadron production. Additionally, I provide evidence of a significant nonjet (NJ) quadrupole ($v_2$) component in $p$-$p$ collisions, with its systematics relating directly to the NJ quadrupole systematics seen in $A$-$A$ collisions. This combination implies that soft, dijet, and NJ quadrupole components are distinct phenomena across all scenarios, suggesting that they are not a result of hadron production from a shared bulk medium exhibiting collective motion (flow).

We examined an alternating Heisenberg spin chain featuring nearest-neighbor ($J_1$) and next-nearest neighbor ($J_2$) antiferromagnetic couplings, as well as the z-component of the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) ($D_z$) interactions. The Hamiltonian was analyzed using (a) Linear Spin-Wave Theory (LSWT) and (b) Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG). Previous reports indicated that the system behaves as a classical ferrimagnet only when nearest-neighbor exchange interactions are present. The inclusion of antiferromagnetic next-nearest neighbor interactions and DM interactions introduces significant quantum fluctuations, which eliminate all signs of ferrimagnetism. We observed that a nonzero $J_2$ causes strong quantum fluctuations at each spin site, resulting in the z-components of both spin-1 and spin-1/2 sites averaging out to zero, leading to a singlet ground state. The combination of $J_1$ and $D_z$ promotes short-range order while establishing long-range order in the XY plane. The interplay between $J_1$ and $J_2$ generates competing phases, as evidenced by the structure factor displaying both sharp and broad peaks at two distinct angles, indicating a local spin spiral structure as well as a lattice configuration. Notably, we discovered that the $D_z$ term eliminates the local spin spiral structure in the z-direction, while facilitating a spiral order in the XY plane.

The natural journey of mammalian sperm is widely recognized as being dependent on their individual motility. In densely populated microenvironments, the progress of any single spermatozoon is likely influenced by its interactions with neighboring swimmers. While the intricate dynamics of individual sperm have been thoroughly examined, the specific effects of hydrodynamic interactions between neighboring cells remain somewhat ambiguous, especially due to the inherent nonlinearity in the hydrodynamic governing principles and the potential variability linked to swimmer morphology. In this study, we aim to clarify the pairwise swimming behaviors of virtual spermatozoa by creating a computational model of an unbound swimming pair and assessing their interactions using a high-accuracy boundary element method. We have investigated a wide range of parameters to analyze the interactions of synchronized spermatozoa, which have frequently been observed in experiments. Our findings indicate that reduced two-dimensional autonomous dynamical systems effectively represent the anisotropic nature of swimming speed and stability that result from near-field hydrodynamic interactions. By focusing on two arrangements of spermatozoa—those positioned side by side and those located above and below each other—we discovered that side-by-side cells exhibit an attractive behavior, with their trajectories in the phase plane accurately modeled by a recently proposed coarse-graining approach for microswimmer dynamics using superposed regularized Stokeslets. In contrast, the above-below arrangement demonstrates a notably stable pairwise swimming behavior, which corresponds to a stable configuration in the plane autonomous system, with the swimmers aligned approximately parallel to one another.

We introduce a continuous-variable (CV) SWAP test that eliminates the need for an ancilla register, extending the concept of the ancilla-free SWAP test used for qubits. In our ancilla-free CV SWAP test, we substitute the computational basis measurement with photon number-resolving measurements. We derive an upper limit on the error in the overlap estimate resulting from a finite Fock cutoff in the detector. For instance, we demonstrate that the overlap estimation of pure, centered, single-mode Gaussian states with energy $E$ that are squeezed in opposite quadratures can be achieved with an error of $\epsilon$ using photon statistics, with a Fock basis cutoff on the order of $O(E \ln \epsilon^{-1})$. This cutoff can be significantly decreased to $E + O(\sqrt{E} \ln \epsilon^{-1})$ when dealing with states that exhibit rapidly decaying Fock tails, such as coherent states. We also explain how the ancilla-free CV SWAP test can be scaled to multiple modes and utilized in quantum algorithms, including variational compiling and entanglement spectroscopy in the CV framework. For entanglement spectroscopy, we introduce a new algorithm that has no counterpart in qubit systems. Finally, we implement the ancilla-free CV SWAP test on Xanadu's 8-mode photonic processor to estimate the vacuum probability of a two-mode squeezed state.

Molecular line observations with various tracers are frequently employed to study the kinematic structure of molecular clouds. However, measurements of cloud velocity dispersions from different lines, even within the same area, often produce inconsistent outcomes. The reasons behind these discrepancies remain somewhat unclear, as molecular line observations can be influenced by several biases. In this study, we examine and clarify the various factors that contribute to linewidth measurement biases by creating synthetic position-position-velocity cubes using a range of tracers derived from a series of self-gravitating magnetohydrodynamic simulations of molecular clouds. We compare the linewidths obtained from these synthetic observations with the actual values in the simulations. Our findings indicate that the differences in linewidth as measured by various tracers are influenced by a combination of density-dependent excitation, where tracers sensitive to higher densities sample smaller regions with lower velocity dispersions, opacity broadening—particularly in highly optically thick tracers like CO—and finite resolution and sensitivity, which can suppress the wings of emission lines. We also discover that, for a fixed signal-to-noise ratio, three widely used tracers—the J=4->3 line of CO, the J=1->0 line of C^{18}O, and the (1,1) inversion transition of NH_3—typically strike a good balance among these competing biases. They provide estimates of velocity dispersion that accurately reflect the true kinematics of a molecular cloud within approximately 10% accuracy, regardless of the cloud's magnetic field strength, evolutionary state, or the orientation of the line of sight relative to the magnetic field.

In this paper, we explore the idea that the anisotropy in the temperature of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) is primarily caused by the primordial fluctuations of QCD-axion-like particles, particularly when the inflaton's perturbations are insufficient to account for the CMB anisotropy. The axion potential derives its energy from radiation, which means that the primordial fluctuations in the axion field during the inflationary period are linked to the CMB anisotropies. As a result, observations impose strict limitations on both the axion model and the scenario of the early universe. The following criteria must be met: (i) the CMB anisotropy must have sufficient amplitude, (ii) it must comply with the axion isocurvature constraint, and (iii) it must adhere to the non-Gaussianity constraint. To fulfill these requirements, a significant energy fraction, denoted as $\Omega_A^{}$, of the axion is necessary at the QCD scale when the axion potential is established. However, it must also diminish considerably in the present era to meet the isocurvature constraint. Therefore, an additional early universe scenario, such as low-scale thermal inflation, is required to dilute the axions after the QCD scale. We examine such a model and determine its permissible parameter space.

Search programs for extra-solar planets rely on precise velocity measurements. They focus on separating radial-velocity changes caused by Doppler motion from the noise generated by stellar activity. We monitored the active K2V star HD 189733 along with its transiting planetary companion, which has an orbital period of 2.2 days. Utilizing the high-resolution spectrograph SOPHIE attached to the 1.93-meter telescope at the Observatoire de Haute-Provence, we collected 55 spectra of HD 189733 over the span of nearly two months. We improved the orbital parameters of HD 189733b and established limits on its eccentricity and a long-term velocity trend. After removing the planet's orbital motion, we analyzed the variability of spectroscopic activity indices in relation to the changes in radial-velocity residuals and the characteristics of the spectral lines. The radial velocity, spectral-line profiles, and activity indices measured in HeI (5875.62 Å), H-alpha (6562.81 Å), and the CaII H&K lines (3968.47 Å and 3933.66 Å) exhibited periodic patterns aligned with the stellar rotation period, and the correlations among these measurements suggest a rotating star with surface spots. We employed these correlations to adjust for the radial-velocity noise attributed to stellar activity, leading to enhanced precision in the orbital parameters. This yielded a semi-amplitude of K = 200.56 ± 0.88 m/s and an estimated planet mass of M_{P} = 1.13 ± 0.03 M$_{Jup}$.

High-Level Synthesis (HLS) tools have achieved considerable success, yet several challenges remain unresolved: 1) the high-level programming abstraction in HLS often obscures potential optimization opportunities; 2) current HLS tools lack flexibility in providing trade-off (Pareto) solutions across various objectives and constraints; 3) it is difficult to accurately predict the quality of the generated RTL designs. To tackle these issues, we introduce an end-to-end framework called IronMan. Its main objective is to facilitate flexible and automated design space exploration (DSE), delivering either optimal solutions based on user-defined constraints or various trade-offs among multiple objectives such as resource types, area, and latency. Achieving such DSE typically requires extensive manual effort or is not feasible with existing HLS tools. IronMan consists of three components: 1) GPP, a precise performance and resource predictor based on graph neural networks; 2) RLMD, a reinforcement-learning-based engine for multi-objective DSE that identifies optimal resource allocation strategies and offers Pareto solutions for different objectives; 3) CT, a code transformer that aids both RLMD and GPP by extracting the data flow graph from original HLS C/C++ code and automatically producing synthesizable code with HLS directives. Experimental results indicate that: 1) GPP achieves significant prediction accuracy, reducing HLS tools' prediction errors by 10.9 times in resource utilization and 5.7 times in timing; 2) RLMD provides optimal or Pareto solutions that outperform genetic algorithms and simulated annealing by 12.7% and 12.9%, respectively; 3) IronMan successfully identifies optimized solutions that meet various DSP constraints, using 2.54 times fewer DSPs and achieving latencies up to 6 times shorter than those of HLS tools, all while operating up to 400 times faster than heuristic algorithms and HLS tools.

We examine price competition in a duopoly scenario with a variable number of buyers. Each seller can offer multiple units of a commodity, with availability being random and potentially varying among sellers. Sellers aim to set prices that attract buyers while ensuring they earn sufficient profits. This pricing decision generally relies on the number of units the seller has as well as the inventory of the competitor, although the seller may only have access to the competitor's statistical data. This framework resembles scenarios in secondary spectrum access networks, non-neutral internet settings, or microgrid networks, where unused spectrum bands, ISP resources, and surplus power units represent the commodities for sale. We analyze this price competition as a strategic game and determine a set of necessary and sufficient conditions for the Nash Equilibrium (NE). Our findings indicate that sellers randomize their pricing using probability distributions with mutually exclusive support sets, arranged in decreasing order based on availability. We demonstrate the uniqueness of a symmetric NE within a symmetric market and provide a detailed calculation of the price distribution in this symmetric NE.

The intricate composition of language presents significant challenges at the intersection of vision and language. However, language can also provide a robust prior that leads to seemingly good performance, even when the underlying models do not fully grasp the visual content. This limitation can impede advancements in the computer vision aspects of multi-modal AI. In this paper, we tackle binary Visual Question Answering (VQA) concerning abstract scenes. We approach this problem as a verification process for the concepts raised in the questions. Specifically, we transform each question into a tuple that succinctly encapsulates the visual concept to be identified within the image. If the concept is present in the image, the answer is "yes"; if not, the answer is "no." Abstract scenes serve two key purposes: (1) they allow us to concentrate on the high-level semantics of the VQA task rather than getting bogged down in low-level recognition issues, and more importantly, (2) they enable us to balance the dataset in a way that controls language priors and emphasizes the role of vision. We gather precise pairs of scenes for each question, ensuring that the answer is "yes" for one scene and "no" for the other when posed with the same question. In fact, relying solely on language priors does not yield better performance than chance on our balanced dataset. Furthermore, our proposed method matches the performance of a leading VQA approach on the unbalanced dataset and surpasses it on the balanced dataset.

We have investigated how the thickness of the Co layer affects magnetocrystalline anisotropy (MCA), the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI), and orbital moment anisotropy (OMA) in W/Co/Pt trilayers to better understand their interrelations. Our findings indicate that the MCA favors magnetization aligned with the film's normal direction and consistently increases as the effective magnetic layer thickness ($t_\mathrm{eff}$) decreases. The Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya exchange constant ($|D|$) also rises as $t_\mathrm{eff}$ decreases, reaching a peak until $t_\mathrm{eff} \approx 1$ nm, after which $|D|$ begins to decline. For $t_\mathrm{eff}$ values greater than approximately 1 nm, both the MCA and $|D|$ exhibit a relationship with $1/t_\mathrm{eff}$, suggesting they originate from the interface. Although the MCA continues to rise as $t_\mathrm{eff}$ falls below approximately 1 nm, this increase occurs at a reduced rate. To determine the reasons behind the $t_\mathrm{eff}$ dependence of MCA and DMI, we examined the OMA of Co in W/Co/Pt trilayers using x-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD). We observed a non-zero OMA for $t_\mathrm{eff}$ values less than about 0.8 nm. This OMA increases more rapidly with decreasing $t_\mathrm{eff}$ than anticipated based on the MCA, implying that other factors besides OMA contribute to the MCA at smaller $t_\mathrm{eff}$. Furthermore, the dependence of OMA on $t_\mathrm{eff}$ suggests that $|D|$ for $t_\mathrm{eff}$ less than approximately 1 nm is not linked to the OMA at the interface. We hypothesize that the growth of Co on W induces strain and/or texture that diminishes both the interfacial DMI and, to some extent, the MCA at smaller $t_\mathrm{eff}$.

We conduct three fiducial Nbody+SPH simulations to investigate the merger of two disk galaxies, each containing a hot gaseous halo, and assess whether the resulting remnant can evolve into a spiral galaxy. During the merging process, the original stellar disks are disrupted by violent relaxation, leading to the formation of a classical bulge from most of the stars, while the rest contribute to a thick disk and its associated bar. A new stellar disk gradually develops in the remnant, primarily from gas accreted from the halo, and it is characterized by being vertically thin and well-extended along its equatorial plane. 

Interestingly, a bar begins to form even before the disk is fully established, which contradicts assumptions made in idealized simulations of isolated bar-forming galaxies. This newly formed bar displays distinct morphological characteristics, including ansae and boxy/peanut bulges, with stars of various ages distributed across different regions of the boxy/peanut structure. Additionally, a disky pseudobulge emerges, resulting in the coexistence of all three bulge types by the conclusion of the simulation. The oldest stars reside in the classical bulge, followed by those in the thick disk, and then the thin disk. The youngest stars are located in the spiral arms and the disky pseudobulge.

The disk surface density profiles exhibit a type II configuration (exponential with a downbending slope), while the circular velocity curves are flat, indicating that the disks in these cases are submaximum: two are clearly so, and one is close to the borderline between maximum and submaximum. On average, only about 10 to 20% of the stellar mass in the final models is contained within the classical bulge, which is significantly less than what was observed in prior simulations.

The spin-orbit interaction of holes in zinc-blende semiconductors is significantly stronger than that of electrons, making hole systems particularly promising for spintronics applications. The three-dimensional (3D) dynamics of holes can be described by the well-known Luttinger Hamiltonian. However, many recent spintronics applications focus on two-dimensional heterostructures, where quantum confinement freezes dynamics in one direction. This confinement leads to a reduction of the Luttinger Hamiltonian from 3D to 2D. The resulting reduction is complex and not fully understood, as it involves the interplay of spin-orbit interaction, an external magnetic field, and the lateral gate potential applied to the heterostructure. In this work, we carry out the dimensional reduction to derive a general effective Hamiltonian that characterizes spintronic effects in symmetric two-dimensional (2D) heterostructures. Specifically, we accomplish the following: (i) derive the spin-orbit interaction and the Darwin interaction associated with the lateral gate potential, (ii) calculate the momentum-dependent out-of-plane g-factor, (iii) identify two independent in-plane g-factors, and (iv) examine the momentum dependencies of the in-plane g-factors.

It is widely accepted that granular media in rapid flow conditions can be represented as a gas of hard spheres with inelastic collisions. At moderate densities, the Enskog kinetic equation, which has been appropriately modified to account for inelastic collisions, serves as a fundamental framework for understanding granular hydrodynamics. A noteworthy finding, in contrast to molecular gases, is that granular mixtures do not achieve energy equipartition, even when in homogeneous states. This indicates that the partial temperatures \(T_i\), which reflect the average kinetic energy of each species, differ from the overall granular temperature \(T\). The purpose of this paper is to explore how varying partial temperatures influence the transport properties of the mixture. Our examination first considers how energy nonequipartition, resulting solely from inelastic collisions, affects transport properties. This phenomenon, absent in elastic collisions, proves significant in key issues related to granular mixtures, such as thermal diffusion segregation. Subsequently, we analyze an additional cause of energy nonequipartition arising from flow velocity divergence. This effect, previously investigated in several pioneering studies on dense hard-sphere molecular mixtures, impacts the bulk viscosity coefficient. We present analytical (approximate) findings alongside Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations, demonstrating the effectiveness of kinetic theory in describing granular flows.

We explore how the initial clumping of ejecta and the effective acceleration of cosmic rays (CRs) influence the density structure of the post-shock region in a Type Ia supernova remnant (SNR) using detailed 3D MHD modeling. Our model simulates the expansion of an SNR within a magnetized interstellar medium (ISM), accounting for the initial clumping of ejecta and the impact of CR acceleration on shock dynamics. We compare our model's predictions with observations from SN 1006. Our results indicate that the back-reaction from accelerated CRs alone fails to explain the observed distance between the forward shock (FS) and the contact discontinuity (CD), unless there are significant energy losses during CR acceleration and escape that are unaffected by the obliquity angle. Conversely, the clumping of ejecta effectively accounts for the small observed separation and the protrusions seen in SN 1006, even in the absence of accelerated CRs. We conclude that the FS-CD separation serves as an indicator of the ejecta structure at the moment of explosion, rather than reflecting the efficiency of CR acceleration in young SNRs.

The universe of Gravitational Waves (GWs) is filled with numerous sources that, if properly studied, could greatly enhance our understanding of the cosmos. By observing massive black hole binaries at high redshifts, we can begin to investigate how seed black holes are formed and track the evolution of galaxies up to the present. Studying extreme mass ratio inspirals will enable us to explore the centers of galaxies in our local universe, test General Relativity, and refine our measurements of Hubble's constant. Detecting compact binaries within our galaxy could provide insights into stellar evolution in the Milky Way. Additionally, identifying cosmic (super)strings and a stochastic background could help constrain cosmological models. Achieving these goals relies not only on our capacity to resolve sources and conduct parameter estimation but also on developing an effective data analysis strategy. In this presentation, I will address the challenges that GW astronomy faces in the context of the ESA L3 Cosmic Vision mission, eLISA.

Newly formed, highly magnetized neutron stars known as magnetars, which are surrounded by their stellar or merger ejecta, are anticipated to be sources of ultrahigh-energy neutrinos. This occurs through the decay of mesons created during hadronic interactions involving protons that are accelerated to ultrahigh energies through the magnetic dissipation of spindown energy. Our analysis indicates that not only pions and kaons, but also charm hadrons—often overlooked because of their low production cross sections—can significantly contribute to neutrino fluence at ultrahigh energies due to their brief lifetimes. In contrast, the production of pions and kaons is diminished at early times since they cool substantially before decaying. We suggest that next-generation detectors like the Probe Of Extreme Multi-Messenger Astrophysics (POEMMA), Giant Radio Array for Neutrino Detection (GRAND), and IceCube-Gen2 are well-positioned to detect neutrinos, primarily sourced from charm hadrons, emitted by nearby magnetars. Furthermore, we propose that neutrinos from magnetar-driven merger novae may be observable within the time frame of $10^2$ s to $10^3$ s, with the charm hadron contribution being dominant for neutrino energies exceeding $10^8$ GeV, which is pertinent for next-generation detectors. Additionally, we discuss the possible effects of charm hadron contributions on the diffuse neutrino flux.

In the Dirac-Milne universe, which is symmetric in terms of matter and antimatter with both components repelling each other, we find that rotation curves are generally flat beyond approximately 3 virial radii. This universe also satisfies a Tully-Fisher relation with an exponent close to 3. Utilizing 3D simulations conducted with a modified RAMSES code, we demonstrate that Dirac-Milne cosmology exhibits a Faber-Jackson relation that has very low scatter and an exponent near 3, relating mass to velocity dispersion. Furthermore, we reveal that the mass calculated from rotation curves under the assumption of Newtonian gravity tends to be systematically overestimated when compared to the actual mass present. Additionally, the Dirac-Milne universe, marked by polarization between its matter and antimatter, behaves similarly to Modified Newtonian Dynamics (MOND), displaying an extra surface gravity relative to the Newtonian framework. We find that at the current epoch, the additional gravitational field intensity, denoted as $g_{am}$, due to antimatter clouds is on the order of a few $10^{-11}$ m/s$^2$, akin to MOND's characteristic acceleration. We investigate how this additional acceleration $g_{am}$ evolves and determine that it varies with redshift, indicating that it is not a fundamental constant. Given its concordance with observed properties related to SNIa luminosity distance, age, nucleosynthesis, and structure formation, Dirac-Milne cosmology may provide an intriguing alternative to $\Lambda$CDM, MOND, and other models aimed at addressing the challenges of Dark Matter and Dark Energy.

We provide a comprehensive overview of an \textit{ab initio} spectral method for determining the energy spectra of two-electron atoms using a hyperspherical coordinate system. In this framework, the Hamiltonian mirrors that of atomic hydrogen, with the Coulomb potential represented in terms of a hyperradius and an angle-dependent effective charge substituting the nuclear charge. The most straightforward spectral approach involves expanding the hyperangular wave function using hyperspherical harmonics. However, this expansion is known to converge very slowly. To address this, we introduce new hyperangular Sturmian functions. While these functions lack an analytical form, they accurately capture the first term of the multipole expansion for the electron-electron interaction potential, specifically the radial electron correlation. We elaborate on the characteristics of these new functions. There are various options for the hyperradius basis functions; in this study, we utilize Coulomb Sturmian functions with half-integer angular momentum. We demonstrate that for H$^-$, the accuracy of the energy levels and the resonance state widths achieved through a single diagonalization of the Hamiltonian is comparable to results from advanced methods, despite using a significantly smaller basis set. Furthermore, we illustrate that precise values for the electric-dipole oscillator strengths of $S \rightarrow P$ transitions in helium are obtained, which validates the accuracy of the bound state wave functions produced by our method.

We analyze a system of two bosons confined in a one-dimensional harmonic trap, interacting through a contact potential. We compare the exact solution of this scenario with a self-consistent numerical solution obtained using the multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree (MCTDH) method. This comparison serves to validate the MCTDH predictions against a few-body problem that can be solved analytically, particularly for the interaction potential most commonly encountered in ultracold quantum gases. Our results indicate that MCTDH effectively captures the exact ground state energy and first-order correlations up to intermediate dimensionless coupling strengths, which correspond to the typical scattering lengths observed in magnetically trapped ultracold dilute Bose gases. However, at larger coupling strengths—often established through methods like Feshbach resonances combined with optical trapping—the MCTDH approach tends to overestimate the depth of the correlation dip in first-order correlations within position space, as well as the degree of fragmentation, defined as the average relative occupancy of orbitals apart from the energetically lowest one. We expect that similar characteristics in the correlation function may emerge with larger particle numbers, setting the stage for a quantitative evaluation of MCTDH accuracy through experiments involving ultracold atoms.

Unitarity is an essential characteristic of any theory that ensures we operate within a theoretically sound framework. Compared to the quark sector, the experimental verification of unitarity for the 3x3 neutrino mixing matrix is notably weaker. It is important to note that most of our knowledge regarding the neutrino mixing angles stems from $\overline{
u}_e$ and $
u_\mu$ disappearance experiments, which rely on the unitarity assumption to limit the remaining matrix elements. New physics could potentially challenge this assumption for the 3x3 matrix, leading to changes in our precision measurements. We conduct a reanalysis to assess how global understanding is affected when oscillation results are refitted without the assumption of unitarity. We present the $3 \sigma$ ranges for the allowed elements of $U_	ext{PMNS}$ that are consistent with all observed data. 

We also determine the limits on the closure of the six neutrino unitarity triangles. The closure of the $
u_e 
u_\mu$ triangle is restricted to $\leq 0.03$, whereas the other triangles are less tightly constrained, with bounds of $\leq 0.1 - 0.2. Additionally, for the row and column normalization, we find that four out of the six such normalizations deviate from unity by $\leq 0.2 - 0.4, while the deviations for the $
u_\mu$ and $
u_e$ row normalization are constrained to $\leq 0.07, all at the $3\sigma$ confidence level. We highlight that there is substantial potential for new low-energy physics, particularly in the $
u_	au$ sector, which is currently constrained by very few experiments.

Standard few-shot relation classification (RC) aims to develop a strong classifier using only a small amount of labeled data for each class. However, prior research has seldom explored how varying the number of classes (denoted as $N$-way) and the number of labeled examples per class (referred to as $K$-shot) impacts training compared to testing. In this study, we introduce a new task called \textit{inconsistent few-shot RC}, in which the model must navigate discrepancies in both $N$ and $K$ between training and testing phases. To tackle this challenge, we propose Prototype Network-based cross-attention contrastive learning (ProtoCACL), which is designed to effectively capture the complex interactions between the support set and query set. Experimental findings show that ProtoCACL surpasses state-of-the-art baseline models in scenarios with both inconsistent $K$ and inconsistent $N$, thanks to its ability to create more robust and discriminative representations. Furthermore, we observe that in the context of inconsistent few-shot learning, models can perform better with \textit{fewer data} compared to the standard few-shot setting, provided that $N$ and $K$ are carefully chosen. Finally, we conclude the paper with additional analyses and recommendations for systematically guiding the selection of $N$ and $K$ in various scenarios.

The Ulysses spacecraft has been in a highly inclined elliptical orbit around the Sun since its encounter with Jupiter in February 1992. Over this period, it has completed nearly three orbits around the Sun. This report focuses on the last three years of data collected by the onboard dust detector. During this time, the detector recorded 609 impacts from dust particles ranging in mass from 10^-16 g to 10^-7 g, contributing to a total of 6,719 dust data sets for the mission. The rate of impacts varied, recording as few as 0.3 impacts per day at high ecliptic latitudes and up to 1.5 impacts per day in the inner solar system. Most impacts detected between 2005 and 2007 appear to originate from interstellar sources, while the remainder are likely from interplanetary sources. 

We compare the interstellar dust measurements from 2005 and 2006 to data gathered during earlier periods (1993/1994 and 1999/2000), when Ulysses passed through the same spatial area at southern ecliptic latitudes, albeit at a different phase of the solar cycle. The impact rate of interstellar grains during these three time frames varied by over a factor of two. Additionally, in the two earlier periods, the direction of grain impacts aligned with the flow direction of interstellar helium. In contrast, the 2005/2006 observations indicated a shift of approximately 30 degrees away from the ecliptic plane in the approach direction of the grains. The cause of this shift is still uncertain but may be related to the configuration of the interplanetary magnetic field during the solar maximum. Our findings also suggest that the dust measurements are consistent with the interplanetary flux model proposed by Staubach et al. (1997), which was created to correspond with five years of Ulysses data.

We propose a conceptual design for a new high-intensity, high-brightness slow-positron production source utilizing a continuous wave electron linac, with an anticipated intensity around 10$^{10}$ e$^+$/s. Achieving this level of intensity in our design depends on the transport of positrons (T$_+$ below 600 keV) from the electron-positron pair production converter target to a low-radiation and low-temperature area, where they are moderated using a high-efficiency cryogenic rare gas moderator made of solid Ne. This design has been developed through Monte Carlo optimizations that focused on various aspects, including electron/positron beam energies, converter target thickness, the transport of the e$^+$ beam to the moderator, extraction of the e$^+$ beam from the magnetic channel, the synchronized raster system, and efficiency calculations for the moderator. For extracting e$^+$ from the magnetic channel, we constructed a prototype for a magnetic field terminator plug, and we present experimental results demonstrating the effectiveness of this prototype. Additionally, we discuss methods for heat dissipation from the converter target and the measures implemented for radiation protection.

The conventional approach to neural language generation utilizes maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) as its optimization technique. From a statistical perspective, MLE effectively minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD) between the actual data distribution and that of the model. However, this method compels the model to allocate non-zero (and often substantial) probability mass to all training samples, regardless of their quality. Furthermore, in its effort to account for the low-probability areas within the data distribution, the model tends to overestimate the likelihood of erroneous text sequences. We believe this is a significant factor contributing to text degeneration during autoregressive decoding. To address this issue, we utilize the total variation distance (TVD), which is less sensitive to outliers, and establish practical bounds for its application in language generation. We then propose the TaiLr objective, which balances the estimation of TVD. Essentially, TaiLr reduces the influence of real data samples that carry low probabilities according to the model, with a customizable penalization intensity. Experimental findings indicate that our approach mitigates the overestimation of degenerated sequences while maintaining diversity, leading to enhanced generation quality across various text generation tasks.

Glasses exhibit solid-like behavior because of their prolonged relaxation time, yet the reason for this slow response remains unclear. It is thought that increasing dynamic length scales from the cooperative motion of particles are key to understanding both the sluggish dynamics and the development of rigidity. In this study, we present experimental findings that demonstrate a growing length scale of dynamic heterogeneity that expands as the waiting time increases in an aging colloidal glass made of Laponite. The evidence of heterogeneity in the dynamics is derived from dynamic light scattering measurements, where we examine both the rotational and translational diffusion of Laponite’s disk-shaped particles in suspension. These measurements are complemented by concurrent microrheology and macroscopic rheology tests. Our results reveal that the rotational diffusion of the particles decreases in speed more rapidly than their translational motion. This decoupling between translational and orientational movements stems from the dynamic heterogeneity, as rotation and translation interact with different length scales in the sample. Additionally, the macroscopic rheology tests indicate that the low-frequency shear viscosity increases at a significantly faster rate compared to both the rotational and translational diffusive relaxation times.

This paper addresses the challenge of dynamically identifying key topics from COVID-19 Tweets and assigning appropriate labels to them in order to provide insights into broader public opinion. In today’s digital age, social media plays a vital role in connecting people through Internet technology and has become an integral part of daily life. In late December 2019, the outbreak of the novel coronavirus, COVID-19, prompted the World Health Organization to declare a global emergency due to its rapid transmission. This epidemic has significantly influenced social media usage worldwide, with Twitter emerging as one of the most prominent platforms, experiencing a surge in activity during the crisis. Consequently, dynamically extracting relevant topics with labels from COVID-19 tweets presents a complex challenge, contrasting with traditional manual topic labeling methods. This paper introduces a framework that automatically identifies key topics and assigns labels using the leading Unigram features from aspect term clusters derived from Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topics. Our experimental results demonstrate that this dynamic approach to topic identification and labeling achieves an accuracy of 85.48%, surpassing that of the manual static method.

The purpose of this paper is to derive the averaged governing equations for non-degenerate oscillatory flows, where the mean velocity and the oscillating velocity have similar magnitudes. We derive these averaged equations for a scalar passive admixture, a vector passive admixture (specifically, the magnetic field in kinematic magnetohydrodynamics), and for vortex dynamics. The small parameter in our asymptotic theory is the inverse dimensionless frequency, denoted as $1/\sigma$. Our mathematical methodology integrates the two-timing method, distinguished limits, and the use of commutators to streamline the calculations. This approach leads to recurrent equations for both the averaged and oscillating components of the unknown fields. We do not rely on any physical or mathematical assumptions beyond the most common ones and provide calculations for the first three successive approximations (zeroth, first, and second). In all examples, the averaged equations reveal a universal structure: terms akin to Reynolds stress (or cross-correlations) are converted into drift velocities, pseudo-diffusion, and two additional terms similar to Moffatt's mean-fields in turbulence. Notably, the averaged behavior of a passive scalar admixture is described solely by drift and pseudo-diffusion. The averaged equations for a passive vectorial admixture and vortex dynamics include two mean-field terms in addition to pseudo-diffusion. It is noteworthy that all mean-field terms (including pseudo-diffusion) are formulated using invariant operators (Lie derivatives), which quantify the deviation of certain tensors from their 'frozen-in' states. Some physical assumptions and findings can be built upon the derived averaged equations. Our physical interpretation indicates that the nature of pseudo-diffusion is purely kinematic.

Measurement-based quantum computing (MBQC) offers a compelling alternative to traditional circuit-based quantum computing, relying on the creation and measurement of cluster states. Recent research has shown that MBQC presents a broader framework for fault tolerance that surpasses the capabilities of foliated quantum error-correcting codes. We systematically build upon this framework and employ combinatorial tiling theory to explore and develop new instances of fault-tolerant cluster states based on crystal structures. Among these is a strong self-dual cluster state that requires only degree-3 connectivity. We evaluate several of these cluster states under circuit-level noise conditions and identify various promising candidates, with their performance varying according to the specific noise model. By eliminating the separation between data and ancilla, this adaptable framework establishes a basis for innovative and competitive fault-tolerance strategies that go beyond traditional error-correcting codes.

We present a technique for the automatic generation of abstract transformers aimed at static analysis through abstract interpretation. This approach emphasizes linear constraints in programs that use rational, real, or floating-point variables, and include linear assignments and tests. By providing the specification of an abstract domain along with a program block, our method generates an implementation of the relevant abstract transformer. This constitutes a type of program transformation. In addition to handling loop-free code, the method can also determine least fixed points as functions of the precondition, enabling the analysis of loops and recursive functions. Our primary focus is on data-flow synchronous programming languages that are employed in developing control-command embedded systems; however, the method is also relevant for imperative and functional programming. Our algorithms leverage techniques of quantifier elimination and symbolic manipulation on linear arithmetic formulas. We additionally provide less general results concerning nonlinear constraints and constructs in nonlinear programming.

We introduce new limitations on the star formation histories of ultra-faint dwarf (UFD) galaxies, utilizing deep photometry from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST). UFDs, a galaxy class recently identified in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, represent a continuation of classical dwarf spheroidals at lower luminosities, providing a fresh perspective on the missing satellite issue. They are the faintest, most dark-matter-dominated, and least chemically-evolved galaxies identified to date. Our HST survey examines six UFDs to assess whether these galaxies serve as true remnants from the early universe. In this report, we present initial findings from three UFD galaxies: Hercules, Leo IV, and Ursa Major I. Unlike classical dwarf spheroidals in the Local Group, which show prolonged star formation histories, these three Milky Way satellites are at least as ancient as the old globular cluster M92 and show no signs of intermediate-age populations. Additionally, their ages seem to be closely aligned, within approximately 1 billion years of each other, which may indicate that their star formation was interrupted by a significant event, such as reionization.

The source of gamma-ray burst (GRB) prompt emissions, which can last from less than a second to several thousand seconds, is still not completely understood despite over 40 years of research. Key uncertainties exist regarding several aspects of GRB physics, such as the composition of jets, energy dissipation processes, mechanisms for particle acceleration, and radiation processes. Recent broad-band observations of prompt emissions by Fermi have intensified discussions in these areas, prompting vigorous theoretical exploration with varying concepts. I will examine these discussions and propose that the current evidence indicates a specific scenario: a quasi-thermal spectral component emerging from the photosphere of the relativistic ejecta has been observed in some GRBs. While this component can dominate the spectrum in certain instances (e.g., GRB 090902B), in the majority of GRBs, it either appears as a minor "shoulder" in the low-energy region of the more prominent "Band" component or is completely undetectable. The primary "Band" spectral component is likely generated in the optically thin region through synchrotron radiation. The variations in magnetization within the GRB central engine may explain the differing prompt emission characteristics seen across various bursts.

Quantum history states have recently been developed by expanding the consistent histories framework proposed by Griffiths to encompass the entangled superposition of evolutionary paths. These states have been tested using Greenberger--Horne--Zeilinger states. The tensor product structure of history-dependent correlations has also been utilized as a quantum computing resource in basic linear optical configurations, demonstrating significant potential in the multiplane diffraction (MPD) of both fermionic and bosonic particles. This advancement encourages the exploration of MPD quantum histories as entanglement resources, which can generate an exponentially growing number of Feynman paths through diffraction planes in a scalable manner, while maintaining a relatively low experimental complexity by integrating coherent light sources with photon-counting detection.

In this article, we refer to quantum temporal correlation and interference among MPD paths as quantum path entanglement (QPE) and quantum path interference (QPI), respectively, recognizing them as innovative quantum resources. We model QPE through operator theory and discuss the counterintuitive characteristics of QPI by integrating history-based formulations with Feynman's path integral methodology. The Leggett--Garg inequality, serving as a temporal counterpart to Bell's inequality, is shown to be violated in the context of MPD, in accordance with the ambiguous form recently introduced by Emary. The proposed theory surrounding MPD-based histories shows great promise for harnessing QPE and QPI as valuable resources for future quantum computation and communication architectures.

Quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) methods for integral estimation are appealing because they generally offer faster convergence rates than pseudo-random Monte Carlo methods. However, implementing them for arbitrary posterior densities in a Bayesian context, particularly for inverse problems, can be challenging. We present a comprehensive parallel Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework and establish both a law of large numbers and a central limit theorem within this context, focusing on non-reversible transitions. Additionally, we expand this framework to include adaptive kernels and identify conditions for maintaining ergodicity. Furthermore, we derive an importance sampling estimator, demonstrating its asymptotic unbiasedness. Our discussion includes the application of completely uniformly distributed (CUD) numbers within these algorithms, resulting in a broad parallel quasi-MCMC (QMCMC) methodology. We prove the consistency of the estimators generated through this approach and provide numerical evidence that it scales close to \( n^{-2} \) with increased parallelization, contrasting with the typical \( n^{-1} \) scaling found in standard MCMC algorithms. In practical statistical models, we observe significant improvements in performance, outpacing pseudo-random methods by multiple orders of magnitude.

In standard practice, the direction of natural gas flow in pipelines is predetermined in advance for simplification and treated as a fixed parameter in scheduling. However, in integrated gas and electric power networks with a significant proportion of intermittent renewable energy sources, it has become increasingly challenging to optimally set these gas flow directions beforehand. Incorrect assumptions about gas flow directions can lead to feasible but suboptimal scheduling. To address this, we introduce a mixed-integer linear optimization model that determines the optimal gas flow directions concurrently with system scheduling. This approach provides enhanced flexibility for power systems, contingent on effective coordination between the power and gas networks. Although this added flexibility increases computational complexity, we assess its value by comparing the overall operational costs of the system using bidirectional gas flows versus unidirectional flows. Our numerical analysis indicates that treating gas flow directions as state variables can offer benefits in both meshed and radial gas networks.

Inflationary cosmology suggests that the anisotropies in the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) provide a way to test predictions of new physics theories. One significant factor at high energy scales is the initial state of quantum fluctuations, which can influence observables like the CMB power spectrum. This study utilizes a quasi-de Sitter inflationary background, featuring an approximate de Sitter mode function based on the Bunch-Davies mode, to explore the scale-dependence of CMB anisotropy. The recent constraints from Planck on the spectral index prompted an investigation into how a new excited mode function, as opposed to a purely de Sitter mode, affects CMB anisotropy at larger angular scales. The results indicate a breaking of angular scale invariance in the temperature fluctuations of the CMB, revealing a slight deviation for $ \ell<200 $.
